title: CDMGTypes
tex_block_html:
A Conditional Directed Mixed Graph (CDMG) $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is called:

  1. Conditional Acyclic Directed Mixed Graph (CADMG) if $G$ is acyclic.
  2. Directed Mixed Graph (DMG) if $J = \emptyset$.
  3. Acyclic Directed Mixed Graph (ADMG) if $G$ is acyclic and $J = \emptyset$.
  4. Conditional Directed Graph (CDG) if $L = \emptyset$.
  5. Directed Graph (DG) if $J = \emptyset$ and $L = \emptyset$.
  6. Conditional Directed Acyclic Graph (CDAG) if $G$ is acyclic and $L = \emptyset$.
  7. Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) if $G$ is acyclic, $J=\emptyset$ and $L = \emptyset$.

tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG in the sense of def def-cdmg. Items i.–vii.\ below introduce seven names; each name is an attribute that $G$ may bear, declared by a clause of the form “$G$ is called iff ”. Every condition is a conjunction drawn from the three atomic conditions

  • “$G$ is acyclic”, the acyclicity predicate of def def-acylic;
  • “$J = \emptyset$”, read as equality of the (finite) input-node set $J$ with the empty set, as elements of the finite-subset type of the underlying node universe of $G$;
  • “$L = \emptyset$”, read as equality of the (finite) bidirected-edge set $L$ with the empty set, as elements of the finite-subset type of $V \times V$ (the ambient type on which $L$ lives per def def-cdmg).

Nested-name semantics. The seven names below do not partition CDMGs. “$G$ is called ” is read as “$G$ satisfies the attribute ”, and a single CDMG $G$ may simultaneously satisfy several of the listed conditions, in which case it simultaneously bears each corresponding name. In particular, whenever the condition of item vii.\ (DAG) holds, so do the conditions of items i.–vi.\ and the base attribute “CDMG” of def def-cdmg, so $G$ then bears all eight names at once.

  1. Conditional Acyclic Directed Mixed Graph (CADMG). $G$ is called a CADMG iff $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic.
  2. Directed Mixed Graph (DMG). $G$ is called a DMG iff $J = \emptyset$.
  3. Acyclic Directed Mixed Graph (ADMG). $G$ is called an ADMG iff $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic and $J = \emptyset$.
  4. Conditional Directed Graph (CDG). $G$ is called a CDG iff $L = \emptyset$.
  5. Directed Graph (DG). $G$ is called a DG iff $J = \emptyset$ and $L = \emptyset$.
  6. Conditional Directed Acyclic Graph (CDAG). $G$ is called a CDAG iff $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic and $L = \emptyset$.
  7. Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). $G$ is called a DAG iff $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic, $J = \emptyset$, and $L = \emptyset$.

Coverage. The three atomic conditions “$G$ is acyclic”, “$J = \emptyset$”, “$L = \emptyset$” are independent boolean predicates on $G$, yielding $2^3 = 8$ possible truth-value tuples. Each of items i.–vii.\ requires a distinct non-empty subset of these three to hold (CADMG: $\{\text{acyclic}\}$; DMG: $\{J = \emptyset\}$; ADMG: $\{\text{acyclic}, J = \emptyset\}$; CDG: $\{L = \emptyset\}$; DG: $\{J = \emptyset, L = \emptyset\}$; CDAG: $\{\text{acyclic}, L = \emptyset\}$; DAG: $\{\text{acyclic}, J = \emptyset, L = \emptyset\}$); the bare CDMG of def def-cdmg (requiring the empty subset, i.e.\ none of the three) accounts for the remaining combination, so the seven named attributes together with the base CDMG attribute exhaust all $2^3 = 8$ boolean combinations. No item constrains the output-node set $V$ or the directed-edge set $E$ beyond what def def-cdmg already requires.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is the file-level helper that binds two implicit parameters for every declaration that follows in the surrounding `namespace`. In Lean 4, `variable` does not itself introduce a definition; it tells the elaborator that any later `def`/`theorem` mentioning `Node` (or needing a `DecidableEq Node` instance) should automatically have those arguments inserted at the front. Here `Node : Type*` is the universe-polymorphic node universe of the graph, and `[DecidableEq Node]` is a typeclass instance that lets Mathlib decide equality between two nodes. The instance is load-bearing because each predicate below either compares two `Finset`s for equality (`G.J = ∅`, `G.L = ∅`, where `Finset` operations dispatch on `DecidableEq`) or invokes `G.IsAcyclic` from `def_3_6`, whose `Walk.IsDirectedWalk` recursion uses `Finset.mem` on `G.E`. Without `DecidableEq Node` none of the predicates would type-check.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Binds Node and DecidableEq Node implicitly for every later decl; load-bearing for Finset/Walk decidability.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code: def IsCADMG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic
lean_blocks.explanation: This block captures the LN's item i: a *Conditional Acyclic Directed Mixed Graph* (CADMG) is a CDMG whose only extra constraint is acyclicity. Of the three atomic conditions in this family — `G.IsAcyclic`, `G.J = ∅`, `G.L = ∅` — only the first is required, so input nodes and bidirected edges remain unrestricted. The predicate is a thin `Prop`-valued alias for `G.IsAcyclic` (from `def_3_6`), packaged under the LN acronym so downstream callers can carry a single `(h : G.IsCADMG)` hypothesis instead of unfolding the acyclicity definition each time. In the naming pattern used by the family (`A` = acyclic, absence of `C` = no input nodes, absence of `M` = no bidirected edges), CADMG occupies the weakest slot above plain CDMG — it is the only predicate of the seven imposing exactly one atomic condition. Since the body is not a conjunction, `rintro` destructuring is not needed; downstream proofs use the hypothesis directly or via `unfold CDMG.IsCADMG`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- CADMG = CDMG that is acyclic; thin Prop alias for G.IsAcyclic from def_3_6, packaged under the LN acronym.
def IsCADMG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic

lean_blocks.code: def IsDMG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.J = ∅
lean_blocks.explanation: This block captures the LN's item ii: a *Directed Mixed Graph* (DMG) is a CDMG with no input/intervention nodes, i.e. the `Finset` field `G.J` is equal to the empty `Finset`. Of the three atomic conditions, only `G.J = ∅` is imposed; cycles and bidirected edges remain unrestricted, so a DMG may still be cyclic and may still carry confounding `L`-edges. The spelling `G.J = ∅` is a literal `Finset Node` equality — Mathlib's `∅` here is `Finset.empty` via the `EmptyCollection` typeclass — chosen to mirror the LN's `J = ∅` notation exactly rather than reformulating as `G.J.card = 0` or a `∀ v, v ∉ G.J` quantifier. In the family-naming convention (absence of the `C` prefix means "no causal/input nodes"), DMG is the bare "no `J`" root that `ADMG` (acyclic + no `J`) and `DAG` (acyclic + no `J` + no `L`) strengthen by adding further conjuncts.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- DMG = CDMG with no input nodes; literal Finset equality G.J = ∅ via Mathlib's EmptyCollection on Finset.
def IsDMG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.J = ∅

lean_blocks.code: def IsADMG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.J = ∅
lean_blocks.explanation: This block captures the LN's item iii: an *Acyclic Directed Mixed Graph* (ADMG) is a CDMG that is both acyclic and has no input nodes. Two of the three atomic conditions are conjoined — `G.IsAcyclic` and `G.J = ∅` — in the LN's stated order (acyclic first), which lets downstream consumers destructure with `rintro ⟨hac, hJ⟩` and read the components back in the same order as the LN. Bidirected edges (`L`) remain unrestricted, so an ADMG may still carry confounding edges — that distinguishes it from `IsCDAG` (acyclic + no `L`) and `IsDAG` (all three). In the family-naming convention `A` = acyclic and absence of `C` = no input nodes, so ADMG sits one conjunct above CADMG and DMG and one conjunct below DAG. ADMG is the workhorse hypothesis of the do-calculus, d-separation, and iSCM chapters: every CBN result quantifies over `IsADMG` graphs.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- ADMG = CDMG that is acyclic and has no input nodes; LN-ordered conjunction unpacks via rintro ⟨hac, hJ⟩.
def IsADMG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.J = ∅

lean_blocks.code: def IsCDG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.L = ∅
lean_blocks.explanation: This block captures the LN's item iv: a *Conditional Directed Graph* (CDG) is a CDMG with no bidirected edges, i.e. the `Finset (Node × Node)` field `G.L` is empty. Only one of the three atomic conditions is imposed; cycles and input nodes remain unrestricted. The spelling `G.L = ∅` is a literal `Finset` equality (Mathlib's `∅` via `EmptyCollection`), matching the LN's `L = ∅` notation verbatim. In the family-naming convention, absence of the `M` (mixed) suffix means "no bidirected edges" — so CDG is the bare "no `L`" root that `CDAG` (acyclic + no `L`), `DG` (no `J` + no `L`), and `DAG` (all three) extend by adding further conjuncts. Practically, ruling out `L` removes latent-confounder edges, leaving only ordinary directed edges — the classical graph shape before mixed-graph causal machinery enters.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- CDG = CDMG with no bidirected edges; literal Finset (Node × Node) equality G.L = ∅ mirrors LN's L = ∅.
def IsCDG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.L = ∅

lean_blocks.code: def IsDG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅
lean_blocks.explanation: This block captures the LN's item v: a *Directed Graph* (DG) is a CDMG with neither input nodes nor bidirected edges, leaving only ordinary directed edges in `G.E`. Two of the three atomic conditions are conjoined — `G.J = ∅` and `G.L = ∅` — in the LN's stated order; downstream callers destructure with `rintro ⟨hJ, hL⟩`. Crucially, acyclicity is *not* required: a `DG` may still contain directed cycles, so the name "directed graph" here is weaker than the colloquial "DAG". In the family-naming convention (absence of `C` = no input nodes, absence of `M` = no bidirected edges), DG sits one conjunct above the single-condition CDG and DMG roots and one conjunct below DAG. Readers expecting "DG implies DAG" should note the implication only runs the other direction; the LN deliberately distinguishes "directed graph" (DG) from "directed acyclic graph" (DAG).
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- DG = CDMG with no inputs and no bidirected edges, but cycles allowed; LN-ordered conjunction G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅.
def IsDG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅

lean_blocks.code: def IsCDAG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.L = ∅
lean_blocks.explanation: This block captures the LN's item vi: a *Conditional Directed Acyclic Graph* (CDAG) is a CDMG that is acyclic and has no bidirected edges, but may still carry input nodes. Two atomic conditions are conjoined — `G.IsAcyclic` and `G.L = ∅` — in the LN's order, so `rintro ⟨hac, hL⟩` reads back in the same sequence. CDAG is the conditional analogue of `DAG`: drop the `G.J = ∅` requirement and a DAG becomes a CDAG. In the family-naming convention (`A` = acyclic, absence of `M` = no bidirected edges, presence of `C` = input nodes still allowed), CDAG sits between `IsCDG` (drop the acyclicity conjunct) and `IsDAG` (add `G.J = ∅` as a third conjunct). It is the right hypothesis whenever a downstream result needs acyclicity and the absence of confounding but should remain expressible on graphs with intervention nodes.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- CDAG = acyclic CDMG with no bidirected edges (input nodes still allowed); conjunction unpacks via rintro ⟨hac, hL⟩.
def IsCDAG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.L = ∅

lean_blocks.code: def IsDAG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅
lean_blocks.explanation: This block captures the LN's item vii: a *Directed Acyclic Graph* (DAG) imposes all three atomic conditions simultaneously — `G.IsAcyclic`, `G.J = ∅`, and `G.L = ∅` — making it the strictest predicate in the family. Any `G` satisfying `IsDAG` automatically satisfies every weaker named attribute (`IsCADMG`, `IsDMG`, `IsADMG`, `IsCDG`, `IsDG`, `IsCDAG`) plus the base `CDMG` attribute, so all 2³ truth-tuples are dominated by DAG in the implication ordering. Lean's `∧` is right-associative, so the body parses as `G.IsAcyclic ∧ (G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅)` and `rintro ⟨hac, hJ, hL⟩` unpacks all three conditions in one move — the LN order is preserved so the destructured names read back as "acyclic, `J = ∅`, and `L = ∅`". `IsDAG` anchors the classical-SCM / Pearlian causality machinery of later chapters and feeds directly into `claim_3_2`, the acyclic ⟺ topological-order equivalence.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- DAG = strictest predicate: acyclic, no inputs, no bidirected edges; right-assoc ∧ unpacks via rintro ⟨hac, hJ, hL⟩.
def IsDAG (G : CDMG Node) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅

design_choices:
**Typing context `Node : Type*` with `[DecidableEq Node]`, inherited from `def_3_1`.** The decidability instance is load-bearing twice over. First, the two `Finset` equalities `G.J = ∅` and `G.L = ∅` decompose under the hood to per-element decidable membership tests — `∅` resolves through the `EmptyCollection` typeclass to `Finset.empty`, and `Finset` equality routes through `DecidableEq` on the element type (`Node` for `J`, `Node × Node` for `L`). Second, the `G.IsAcyclic` predicate reused from `def_3_6` transitively needs the same instance, because its body's `Walk`, `Walk.IsDirectedWalk`, and `v ∈ G` machinery all bottom out in `Finset.mem` on `G.E` and `G.J ∪ G.V`. Dropping the typeclass would make every one of the seven predicates fail to type-check. The line uses the three-dash `--- start helper` marker rather than the two-dash `-- start statement` flavour reserved for declarations whose body is formalised LN content: a `variable` line is statement-typing infrastructure binding the implicit parameters the seven predicates then reference, not LN material in its own right. The convention is shared across `CDMG.lean`, `CDMGNotation.lean`, `Walks.lean`, `EdgeRelations.lean`, `CDMGRestrictions.lean`, and `Acyclicity.lean`.

**Seven independent `Prop` predicates on the single base type `CDMG Node`, not an `inductive CDMGType` enum, bundled structure subtypes, or typeclasses.** The LN's "is called" semantics are *nested membership*: a graph satisfying the DAG conjunction (item vii) simultaneously bears each of the weaker names CADMG, DMG, ADMG, CDG, DG, CDAG plus the base `CDMG` attribute of `def_3_1` — all eight of the $2^3$ truth-tuples are "$\le$" DAG in the implication ordering. An `inductive` encoding would force exactly one constructor per graph and could not express this overlap. A bundled structure subtype `structure ADMG (Node) extends CDMG Node where acyclic : … ; noInput : …` would split the type universe: every downstream site that mixes ADMG-shaped hypotheses with general CDMG infrastructure would need a forgetful `ADMG → CDMG` coercion, and every `Membership`, `Walk`, and `IsAcyclic` lemma proved on `CDMG Node` would have to be re-derived or transported on `ADMG Node`. A typeclass `IsDAG G` would invite Lean's instance search to fire opportunistically and is the wrong tool — these are mathematical attributes a *specific* graph either bears or does not, not structure-of-the-type-itself in the way `Group` or `LinearOrder` are. Seven `Prop`-valued predicates on the single base type capture the overlap on the nose, and because each predicate lives on the same `CDMG Node` the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance, the `Walk` inductive, the `Walk.IsDirectedWalk` predicate, and `IsAcyclic` itself all transport verbatim — a walk lemma proved on a `CDMG` is immediately available for ADMGs, DAGs, and the rest, with no `Subtype.val` unwrapping, no coercion lemmas, no parallel `IsAcyclic_ADMG` re-definitions. The seven names are pure *vocabulary* layered over the single base type.

**Literal `∧`-conjunction of `G.IsAcyclic`, `G.J = ∅`, `G.L = ∅` in LN order, written `def` not `abbrev`.** LN item iii reads "acyclic and $J = \emptyset$"; the Lean text encodes it as `G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.J = ∅`, in that order, never the reverse — and likewise for items v, vi, vii. Logically the order is irrelevant, but preserving it means `obtain ⟨hac, hJ⟩ := h` reads back as "acyclic and `J = ∅`" without reshuffling against the LN. Lean's `∧` is right-associative, so the three-conjunct body of `IsDAG` parses as `G.IsAcyclic ∧ (G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅)` and `obtain ⟨hac, hJ, hL⟩ := h` unpacks all three atomic conditions in one move. A more abstract algebraic combinator — a `Finset`-indexed conjunction, or a custom `All [acyclic, noInput, noBidirected]` predicate — would only obscure the LN's plain wording for no proof-engineering gain; seven cases are few enough that `∧` written out is clearest. `def` rather than `abbrev` preserves the named abstraction: an `abbrev` would auto-unfold at every elaboration site, erasing `IsDAG` from goals; consumers who want the conjunction explicit can `unfold CDMG.IsDAG` (or `simp only [CDMG.IsDAG]`) on demand. This pays off in chapters 4–10, where do-calculus, $d$-separation, and the iSCM developments quantify over `IsADMG` graphs and Pearlian-causality results take `(h : G.IsDAG)` directly. `G.J = ∅` and `G.L = ∅` are kept as literal `Finset` equalities, never as cardinality (`G.J.card = 0`) or quantifier (`∀ v, v ∉ G.J`) reformulations, because the LN writes $J = \emptyset$ verbatim and a reformulation would force a one-line bridge lemma at every downstream site that case-splits on emptiness. One LN subtlety: `IsDG` (item v) is `G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅` only — *no* acyclicity conjunct, because a "directed graph" without the "acyclic" qualifier is allowed to contain directed cycles. A reader expecting "DG implies DAG" is wrong; the implication only runs the other direction.

**Naming convention `Is…` matches mathlib; mathlib's own subtype zoo cannot be re-used.** `IsCADMG`, `IsDMG`, `IsADMG`, `IsCDG`, `IsDG`, `IsCDAG`, `IsDAG` are the LN acronyms verbatim with an `Is` prefix, mirroring `IsAcyclic` from `def_3_6` and mathlib's broader convention (`IsAcyclic`, `IsDirected`, `IsConnected`, …). Capitalisation tracks the LN acronyms exactly so a reader can grep `CADMG` / `ADMG` / `DAG` from the notes and land on the Lean counterpart with no translation table; declaration order follows LN items i–vii. Mathlib's own subtype zoo around `SimpleGraph` and `DiGraph` does not help: `SimpleGraph.IsAcyclic`, `SimpleGraph.IsTree`, and the `Quiver` / `DiGraph` predicates are all built on undirected single-channel graphs or simple categorical quivers — none carries the CDMG's $J$/$V$ input/output partition, none distinguishes ordinary directed edges $E$ from bidirected confounders $L$, and none admits the "acyclic only as a directed-walk constraint while bidirected walks are unconstrained" semantics that `def_3_6` pins down. Retargeting any mathlib predicate would first require re-deriving the entire CDMG infrastructure underneath it. No `Decidable` instances are exposed here: each predicate is a conjunction of `G.IsAcyclic` (whose decidability is deferred per `Acyclicity.lean`'s design block) and `Finset` equalities (decidable in principle, but only useful when `IsAcyclic` is too), so decidability plumbing is deferred to the causal-discovery algorithms in later chapters that actually consume it.
